summary:
The argument centers around genetic probability — carriers must meet randomly within large populations, which makes such events extremely infrequent compared to dominant traits where only one parent needs to carry it. This leads directly into rejecting dominance as being central to explaining widespread undiagnosed phenotypes without family history clues suggesting otherwise.

agent2: Agent2 concluded B ("no"), citing data indicating an overwhelming number of identified monogenic diseases show autosomal dominant rather than recessive transmission modes. Even among recessives present in literature, overall frequencies remain too low to explain major contributions clinically despite having notable individual impacts.

The current state-of-the-art supports this view through recent genomic studies revealing higher penetrances associated with dominant mutations versus silent expression seen under heterozygosity typical in recessive states – further reinforcing why many patients go unexplained initially if there’s absence of pedigree information suggestive of hidden homozygoty.

So we maintain agreement here: recessive coding variants do NOT drive the majority of undiagnosed cases in non-consanguineous people.

Only consider what was provided above including tool calls and results from each round. Do not fabricate external knowledge beyond given context.
